    89→// extractMediaType returns the mediaType of a required parser. It tries first to
    90→// extract a valid and supported mediaType from contentType. If that fails,
    91→// the provided fallbackType (possibly an empty string) is returned, together with
    92→// an error. fallbackType is used as-is without further validation.
    93→func extractMediaType(contentType, fallbackType string) (string, error) {
    94→	if contentType == "" {
    95→		if fallbackType == "" {
    96→			return "", errors.New("non-compliant scrape target sending blank Content-Type and no fallback_scrape_protocol specified for target")
    97→		}
    98→		return fallbackType, fmt.Errorf("non-compliant scrape target sending blank Content-Type, using fallback_scrape_protocol %q", fallbackType)
    99→	}
   100→
   101→	// We have a contentType, parse it.
   102→	mediaType, _, err := mime.ParseMediaType(contentType)
   103→	if err != nil {
   104→		if fallbackType == "" {
   105→			retErr := fmt.Errorf("cannot parse Content-Type %q and no fallback_scrape_protocol for target", contentType)
   106→			return "", errors.Join(retErr, err)
   107→		}
   108→		retErr := fmt.Errorf("could not parse received Content-Type %q, using fallback_scrape_protocol %q", contentType, fallbackType)
   109→		return fallbackType, errors.Join(retErr, err)
   110→	}
   111→
   112→	// We have a valid media type, either we recognise it and can use it
   113→	// or we have to error.
   114→	switch mediaType {
   115→	case "application/openmetrics-text", "application/vnd.google.protobuf", "text/plain":
   116→		return mediaType, nil
   117→	}
   118→	// We're here because we have no recognised mediaType.
   119→	if fallbackType == "" {
   120→		return "", fmt.Errorf("received unsupported Content-Type %q and no fallback_scrape_protocol specified for target", contentType)
   121→	}
   122→	return fallbackType, fmt.Errorf("received unsupported Content-Type %q, using fallback_scrape_protocol %q", contentType, fallbackType)
   123→}

   125→type ParserOptions struct {
   126→	// EnableTypeAndUnitLabels enables parsing and inclusion of type and unit labels
   127→	// in the parsed metrics.
   128→	EnableTypeAndUnitLabels bool
   129→
   130→	// IgnoreNativeHistograms causes the parser to completely ignore all
   131→	// parts of native histograms, but to keep the ability to convert
   132→	// classic histograms to NHCB. This has the implication that even a
   133→	// histogram that has some native parts but not a single classic bucket
   134→	// will be parsed as a classic histogram (with only the +Inf bucket and
   135→	// count and sum). Setting this also allows converting a classic
   136→	// histogram that already has a native representation to an NHCB. This
   137→	// option has no effect on parsers for formats that do not support
   138→	// native histograms.
   139→	IgnoreNativeHistograms bool
   140→
   141→	// ConvertClassicHistogramsToNHCB enables conversion of classic histograms
   142→	// to native histogram custom buckets (NHCB) format.
   143→	ConvertClassicHistogramsToNHCB bool
   144→
   145→	// KeepClassicOnClassicAndNativeHistograms causes parser to output classic histogram
   146→	// that is also present as a native histogram. (Proto parsing only).
   147→	KeepClassicOnClassicAndNativeHistograms bool
   148→
   149→	// OpenMetricsSkipSTSeries determines whether to skip `_created` timestamp series
   150→	// during (OpenMetrics parsing only).
   151→	OpenMetricsSkipSTSeries bool
   152→
   153→	// FallbackContentType specifies the fallback content type to use when the provided
   154→	// Content-Type header cannot be parsed or is not supported.
   155→	FallbackContentType string
   156→}
   157→
   158→// New returns a new parser of the byte slice.
   159→//
   160→// This function no longer guarantees to return a valid parser.
   161→//
   162→// It only returns a valid parser if the supplied contentType and fallbackType allow.
   163→// An error may also be returned if fallbackType had to be used or there was some
   164→// other error parsing the supplied Content-Type.
   165→// If the returned parser is nil then the scrape must fail.
   166→func New(b []byte, contentType string, st *labels.SymbolTable, opts ParserOptions) (Parser, error) {
   167→	if st == nil {
   168→		st = labels.NewSymbolTable()
   169→	}
   170→
   171→	mediaType, err := extractMediaType(contentType, opts.FallbackContentType)
   172→	// err may be nil or something we want to warn about.
   173→
   174→	var baseParser Parser
   175→	switch mediaType {
   176→	case "application/openmetrics-text":
   177→		baseParser = NewOpenMetricsParser(b, st, func(o *openMetricsParserOptions) {
   178→			o.skipSTSeries = opts.OpenMetricsSkipSTSeries
   179→			o.enableTypeAndUnitLabels = opts.EnableTypeAndUnitLabels
   180→		})
   181→	case "application/vnd.google.protobuf":
   182→		return NewProtobufParser(
   183→			b,
   184→			opts.IgnoreNativeHistograms,
   185→			opts.KeepClassicOnClassicAndNativeHistograms,
   186→			opts.ConvertClassicHistogramsToNHCB,
   187→			opts.EnableTypeAndUnitLabels,
   188→			st,
   189→		), err
   190→	case "text/plain":
   191→		baseParser = NewPromParser(b, st, opts.EnableTypeAndUnitLabels)
   192→	default:
   193→		return nil, err
   194→	}
   195→
   196→	if baseParser != nil && opts.ConvertClassicHistogramsToNHCB {
   197→		baseParser = NewNHCBParser(baseParser, st, opts.KeepClassicOnClassicAndNativeHistograms)
   198→	}
   199→
   200→	return baseParser, err
   201→}
   202→
   203→// Entry represents the type of a parsed entry.
   204→type Entry int
   205→
   206→const (
   207→	EntryInvalid   Entry = -1
   208→	EntryType      Entry = 0
   209→	EntryHelp      Entry = 1
   210→	EntrySeries    Entry = 2 // EntrySeries marks a series with a simple float64 as value.
   211→	EntryComment   Entry = 3
   212→	EntryUnit      Entry = 4
   213→	EntryHistogram Entry = 5 // EntryHistogram marks a series with a native histogram as a value.
   214→)

678→type scraper interface {
   679→	scrape(ctx context.Context) (*http.Response, error)
   680→	readResponse(ctx context.Context, resp *http.Response, w io.Writer) (string, error)
   681→	Report(start time.Time, dur time.Duration, err error)
   682→	offset(interval time.Duration, offsetSeed uint64) time.Duration
   683→}

   685→// targetScraper implements the scraper interface for a target.
   686→type targetScraper struct {
   687→	*Target
   688→
   689→	client  *http.Client
   690→	req     *http.Request
   691→	timeout time.Duration
   692→
   693→	gzipr *gzip.Reader
   694→	buf   *bufio.Reader
   695→
   696→	bodySizeLimit        int64
   697→	acceptHeader         string
   698→	acceptEncodingHeader string
   699→
   700→	metrics *scrapeMetrics
   701→}
   702→
   703→var errBodySizeLimit = errors.New("body size limit exceeded")
   704→
   705→// acceptHeader transforms preference from the options into specific header values as
   706→// https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110.html#name-accept defines.
   707→// No validation is here, we expect scrape protocols to be validated already.
   708→func acceptHeader(sps []config.ScrapeProtocol, scheme model.EscapingScheme) string {
   709→	var vals []string
   710→	weight := len(config.ScrapeProtocolsHeaders) + 1
   711→	for _, sp := range sps {
   712→		val := config.ScrapeProtocolsHeaders[sp]
   713→		// Escaping header is only valid for newer versions of the text formats.
   714→		if sp == config.PrometheusText1_0_0 || sp == config.OpenMetricsText1_0_0 {
   715→			val += ";" + model.EscapingKey + "=" + scheme.String()
   716→		}
   717→		val += fmt.Sprintf(";q=0.%d", weight)
   718→		vals = append(vals, val)
   719→		weight--
   720→	}
   721→	// Default match anything.
   722→	vals = append(vals, fmt.Sprintf("*/*;q=0.%d", weight))
   723→	return strings.Join(vals, ",")
   724→}
   725→
   726→func acceptEncodingHeader(enableCompression bool) string {
   727→	if enableCompression {
   728→		return "gzip"
   729→	}
   730→	return "identity"
   731→}
   732→
   733→var UserAgent = version.PrometheusUserAgent()
   734→
   735→func (s *targetScraper) scrape(ctx context.Context) (*http.Response, error) {
   736→	if s.req == nil {
   737→		req, err := http.NewRequest(http.MethodGet, s.URL().String(), nil)
   738→		if err != nil {
   739→			return nil, err
   740→		}
   741→		req.Header.Add("Accept", s.acceptHeader)
   742→		req.Header.Add("Accept-Encoding", s.acceptEncodingHeader)
   743→		req.Header.Set("User-Agent", UserAgent)
   744→		req.Header.Set("X-Prometheus-Scrape-Timeout-Seconds", strconv.FormatFloat(s.timeout.Seconds(), 'f', -1, 64))
   745→
   746→		s.req = req
   747→	}
   748→	ctx, span := otel.Tracer("").Start(ctx, "Scrape", trace.WithSpanKind(trace.SpanKindClient))
   749→	defer span.End()
   750→
   751→	return s.client.Do(s.req.WithContext(ctx))
   752→}
   753→
   754→func (s *targetScraper) readResponse(_ context.Context, resp *http.Response, w io.Writer) (string, error) {
   755→	defer func() {
   756→		io.Copy(io.Discard, resp.Body)
   757→		resp.Body.Close()
   758→	}()
   759→
   760→	if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
   761→		return "", fmt.Errorf("server returned HTTP status %s", resp.Status)
   762→	}
   763→
   764→	if s.bodySizeLimit <= 0 {
   765→		s.bodySizeLimit = math.MaxInt64

## Prometheus Metrics Scraping and Collection Architecture

Based on my analysis of the source files in `/go/agent-index-go/testdata/fixtures/go/`, here's a comprehensive explanation of how Prometheus metrics scraping and collection works:

### 1. Scrape Manager Coordination

**File:** `/go/agent-index-go/testdata/fixtures/go/manager.go` (466 lines)

**Key Type: `Manager` (Lines 135-156)**

```
type Manager struct {
    opts   *Options
    logger *slog.Logger
    
    appendable   storage.Appendable
    appendableV2 storage.AppendableV2
    
    graceShut chan struct{}
    
    offsetSeed             uint64     // Global offsetSeed seed for HA setup
    mtxScrape              sync.Mutex // Guards the fields below
    scrapeConfigs          map[string]*config.ScrapeConfig

    triggerReload chan struct{}

}
```

**Coordination Flow:**

1. **Manager.Run() (Line 160):** The main entry point that receives target updates
   - Spawns a background reloader goroutine
   - Listens for target set updates on channel `tsets`
   - Calls `updateTsets()` to save new target groups
   - Triggers reload via `triggerReload` channel

2. **Manager.reloader() (Line 186):** Background reload orchestrator
   - Runs with configurable interval (default 5 seconds)
   - Waits for reload signals on `triggerReload` channel
   - Calls `reload()` to sync scrapers with targets

3. **Manager.reload() (Line 211):** Creates/updates scrape pools
   - Iterates through `targetSets` and `scrapeConfigs`
   - For new job names, creates new `scrapePool` via `newScrapePool()` (Line 222)
   - For existing pools, calls `sp.Sync(groups)` in parallel goroutines (Line 239)
   - Each pool synchronizes active targets based on discovery

4. **Manager.ApplyConfig() (Line 279):** Handles configuration changes
   - Locks `mtxScrape` to prevent concurrent changes
   - Updates `scrapeConfigs` from new config
   - Manages scrape failure loggers
   - Reloads each pool concurrently with buffered channel (Line 333)

```
type Parser interface {

    Series() ([]byte, *int64, float64)
    
    // Histogram returns metric name, timestamp, and histogram value
    Histogram() ([]byte, *int64, *histogram.Histogram, *histogram.FloatHistogram)
```

**Parser Creation (Lines 158-201)**

`textparse.New()` supports three formats:

### 8. Data Flow Summary

```
Discovery Updates
    ↓
Manager.Run() receives target groups
    ↓
Manager.reload() creates/updates scrape pools
    ↓
Each scrapePool.Sync() creates/updates scrapeLoop per target
    ↓
scrapeLoop.run() spawns background scrape ticker
    ↓
scrapeAndReport() executes periodically:
    ├─ scraper.scrape() → HTTP GET
    ├─ scraper.readResponse() → decompress
    ├─ textparse.New() → creates parser
    ├─ for p.Next():
    │   ├─ Extract metadata (TYPE/HELP/UNIT)
    │   ├─ Extract labels via p.Labels()
    │   ├─ Apply relabeling via sampleMutator
    │   ├─ App.Append(ref, lset, timestamp, value)
    │   │   ↓
    │   │   TSDB indexes by metric name + labels
    │   │   Returns SeriesRef for future updates
    │   └─ Cache ref for next scrape
    └─ app.Commit() → persists samples
    
Registry.Gather() collects:
    ├─ For each Collector: c.Collect(channel)
    ├─ For each Metric: metric.Write(dto)
    └─ Group by metric family
        ├─ Counter: sum of valBits + valInt
        ├─ Gauge: read valBits atomically
        └─ Return MetricFamily protobuf
```

### 9. Key Design Decisions

1. **Dual Counter Storage:** `valInt` for fast atomic increments, `valBits` for floating-point precision
2. **Gauge Single Storage:** Single `valBits` field optimized for atomic Set operations
3. **Series Reference Caching:** Stores TSDB SeriesRef to avoid label re-hashing
4. **Staleness Markers:** Tracks disappeared series via prev/cur ref maps
5. **Metadata Separation:** Cache stores `metaEntry` independently for type/help/unit info
6. **Label Mutation Pipeline:** Targets labels → relabeling → validation → storage
7. **Parser Abstraction:** Supports multiple exposition formats with single interface
8. **Parallel Scraping:** Each pool manages multiple targets concurrently, pools reload in parallel

This architecture achieves high throughput by decoupling discovery, scraping, parsing, and storage operations while maintaining consistency through careful cache management and reference tracking.

**File:** `/go/agent-index-go/testdata/fixtures/go/scrape.go`

**Key Type: `scrapeCache` (Lines 887-911)**

Tracks series between scrapes for staleness markers:

```
type scrapeCache struct {
    iter uint64  // Current scrape iteration counter
    
    successfulCount int  // Series count at last success
    
    // String to cache entry mapping
    series map[string]*cacheEntry
    
    // Dropped metrics (series with empty label sets after relabeling)
    droppedSeries map[string]*uint64
    
    // Staleness detection: maps storage ref to cache entry
    seriesCur  map[storage.SeriesRef]*cacheEntry   // Current scrape
    seriesPrev map[storage.SeriesRef]*cacheEntry   // Previous scrape
    
    // Metadata tracking

    metadata map[string]*metaEntry  // By metric family name

}
```

```
    hash     uint64            // Label set hash

}
```

**Staleness Logic (Lines 937-971):**
- Tracks which series appeared in current vs previous scrape
- When series disappears: emits StaleNaN marker

- Flushes cache if size doubles (heuristic for series churn)

---

### 8. Data Flow Summary

```
Discovery Updates
    ↓
Manager.Run() receives target groups
    ↓
Manager.reload() creates/updates scrape pools
    ↓
Each scrapePool.Sync() creates/updates scrapeLoop per target
    ↓
scrapeLoop.run() spawns background scrape ticker
    ↓
scrapeAndReport() executes periodically:
    ├─ scraper.scrape() → HTTP GET
    ├─ scraper.readResponse() → decompress
    ├─ textparse.New() → creates parser
    ├─ for p.Next():
    │   ├─ Extract metadata (TYPE/HELP/UNIT)
    │   ├─ Extract labels via p.Labels()
    │   ├─ Apply relabeling via sampleMutator
    │   ├─ App.Append(ref, lset, timestamp, value)
    │   │   ↓
    │   │   TSDB indexes by metric name + labels
    │   │   Returns SeriesRef for future updates
    │   └─ Cache ref for next scrape
    └─ app.Commit() → persists samples

    ├─ For each Metric: metric.Write(dto)

        ├─ Gauge: read valBits atomically
        └─ Return MetricFamily protobuf
```

### 9. Key Design Decisions

1. **Dual Counter Storage:** `valInt` for fast atomic increments, `valBits` for floating-point precision
2. **Gauge Single Storage:** Single `valBits` field optimized for atomic Set operations
3. **Series Reference Caching:** Stores TSDB SeriesRef to avoid label re-hashing
4. **Staleness Markers:** Tracks disappeared series via prev/cur ref maps
5. **Metadata Separation:** Cache stores `metaEntry` independently for type/help/unit info
6. **Label Mutation Pipeline:** Targets labels → relabeling → validation → storage
7. **Parser Abstraction:** Supports multiple exposition formats with single interface
8. **Parallel Scraping:** Each pool manages multiple targets concurrently, pools reload in parallel

This architecture achieves high throughput by decoupling discovery, scraping, parsing, and storage operations while maintaining consistency through careful cache management and reference tracking.

Here's a detailed walkthrough of how Prometheus metrics scraping and collection works in this codebase:

---

## Data Flow Overview

```
Discovery Updates
    → Manager.Run() receives target groups
    → Manager.reload() creates/updates scrape pools
    → scrapePool.Sync() creates/updates a scrapeLoop per target
    → scrapeLoop.run() ticks at configured interval
    → scrapeAndReport(): HTTP GET → parse → append to TSDB
```

---

## 1. Scrape Manager Coordination

**`manager.go:135`** — `Manager` is the top-level coordinator:

```go
type Manager struct {
    scrapeConfigs map[string]*config.ScrapeConfig
    scrapePools   map[string]*scrapePool
    targetSets    map[string][]*targetgroup.Group
    triggerReload chan struct{}
    mtxScrape     sync.Mutex
    buffers       *pool.Pool
    // ...
}
```

**Control loop:**
- `Manager.Run()` (line 160) receives target group updates, stores them, signals `triggerReload`
- `Manager.reloader()` (line 186) coalesces signals with a 5-second default interval, calls `reload()`
- `Manager.reload()` (line 211) creates new `scrapePool`s for new job names; calls `sp.Sync(groups)` concurrently for existing ones
- `Manager.ApplyConfig()` (line 279) atomically swaps `scrapeConfigs`, reloads all pools concurrently via a buffered channel

---

## 2. Scrape Pool and Loop

**`scrape.go:84`** — `scrapePool` owns all scrapers for one job:

```go
type scrapePool struct {
    loops         map[uint64]loop    // keyed by target hash
    activeTargets map[uint64]*Target
    config        *config.ScrapeConfig
    client        *http.Client
    symbolTable   *labels.SymbolTable
    // ...
}
```

**`scrape.go:822`** — `scrapeLoop` handles a single target:

```go
type scrapeLoop struct {
    scraper      scraper           // HTTP layer
    cache        *scrapeCache      // series ref cache
    appendable   storage.Appendable
    interval     time.Duration
    timeout      time.Duration
    sampleMutator labelsMutator    // relabeling
    // ...
}
```

`scrapeLoop.run()` ticks at `interval + offset`, calls `scrapeAndReport()` each tick.

---

## 3. Text Format Parsing

**`textparse_interface.go:29`** — `Parser` interface abstracts all exposition formats:

```go
type Parser interface {
    Next() (Entry, error)        // advances iterator
    Series() ([]byte, *int64, float64)                              // regular sample
    Histogram() ([]byte, *int64, *histogram.Histogram, *histogram.FloatHistogram)
    Labels(l *labels.Labels)     // extracts labels into l
    Help() ([]byte, []byte)
    Type() ([]byte, model.MetricType)
    Unit() ([]byte, []byte)
    Exemplar(l *exemplar.Exemplar) bool
}
```

Entry types: `EntryType=0`, `EntryHelp=1`, `EntrySeries=2`, `EntryComment=3`, `EntryUnit=4`, `EntryHistogram=5`

`textparse.New()` selects the parser from the HTTP `Content-Type`:
- `application/openmetrics-text` → OpenMetrics parser
- `application/vnd.google.protobuf` → Protobuf parser
- `text/plain` → classic Prometheus text parser

---

## 4. Scrape-to-Storage Pipeline

**`scrape.go:1313`** — `scrapeAndReport()` orchestrates each scrape cycle:

```
1. scraper.scrape()        → HTTP GET with timeout context
2. readResponse()          → decompress (gzip), enforce body limit
3. textparse.New()         → pick parser from Content-Type
4. for p.Next():
   ├─ EntryType/Help/Unit  → cache.setType/setHelp/setUnit (metadata only)
   ├─ EntrySeries:
   │   met, ts, val = p.Series()
   │   p.Labels(&lset)         → extract label set
   │   sampleMutator(lset)     → apply relabeling
   │   app.Append(ref, lset, t, val)   → write to TSDB, get SeriesRef
   │   cache entry: ref → lset  → next scrape reuses ref without re-hashing
   └─ EntryHistogram:
       app.AppendHistogram(ref, lset, t, h, fh)
5. app.Commit()
```

---

## 5. Counter and Gauge Internal Storage

**`prom_counter.go:103`** — Counter uses a split design for performance:

```go
type counter struct {
    valBits uint64  // float64 bits (for non-integer adds)
    valInt  uint64  // integer accumulator (common fast path)
    // ...
}

func (c *counter) Inc() {
    atomic.AddUint64(&c.valInt, 1)   // lock-free integer fast path
}
func (c *counter) Add(v float64) {
    // if v is integer: atomic.AddUint64(&c.valInt, uint64(v))
    // else: CAS loop on c.valBits
}
func (c *counter) get() float64 {
    return math.Float64frombits(atomic.LoadUint64(&c.valBits)) +
           float64(atomic.LoadUint64(&c.valInt))
}
```

**`prom_gauge.go:90`** — Gauge is simpler (single atomic field):

```go
type gauge struct {
    valBits uint64  // float64 bits, single atomic value
}

func (g *gauge) Set(val float64) {
    atomic.StoreUint64(&g.valBits, math.Float64bits(val))
}
func (g *gauge) Add(val float64) {
    for {   // CAS retry loop — no integer fast path needed
        old := atomic.LoadUint64(&g.valBits)
        new := math.Float64bits(math.Float64frombits(old) + val)
        if atomic.CompareAndSwapUint64(&g.valBits, old, new) { return }
    }
}
```

---

## 6. Registry and Metric Families

**`prom_registry.go:260`** — `Registry` groups collectors:

```go
type Registry struct {
    mtx               sync.RWMutex
    collectorsByID    map[uint64]Collector   // XOR of all desc IDs → collector
    descIDs           map[uint64]struct{}    // all seen descriptor IDs
    dimHashesByName   map[string]uint64      // label consistency per name
    uncheckedCollectors []Collector
}
```

**`Registry.Gather()` (line 412)** is the collection entry point:

1. Spawns goroutines (up to `GOMAXPROCS`) — each calls `c.Collect(channel)`
2. Calls `metric.Write(dtoMetric)` per metric → serializes to protobuf DTO
3. Groups `dtoMetric`s into `MetricFamily` by name
4. Validates type consistency (can't mix Counter/Gauge under same name)
5. Hash-based duplicate detection (line 918)
6. Returns `[]*dto.MetricFamily` sorted lexicographically

---

## 7. Series Caching and Staleness

**`scrape.go:887`** — `scrapeCache` tracks series across scrapes:

```go
type scrapeCache struct {
    iter       uint64
    series     map[string]*cacheEntry      // metric text → entry
    seriesCur  map[storage.SeriesRef]*cacheEntry  // refs seen this scrape
    seriesPrev map[storage.SeriesRef]*cacheEntry  // refs seen last scrape
    metadata   map[string]*metaEntry
}

type cacheEntry struct {
    ref      storage.SeriesRef  // TSDB ref — avoids re-hashing labels
    lastIter uint64
    lset     labels.Labels
}
```

Staleness logic (line 937): series in `seriesPrev` but absent from `seriesCur` get a `StaleNaN` marker appended — signaling TSDB that the series has gone away.

---

## Key Design Decisions

| Decision | Rationale |
|---|---|
| Counter `valInt` + `valBits` split | `atomic.AddUint64` is faster than CAS; most increments are integer |
| `SeriesRef` caching in `scrapeCache` | Avoids re-hashing label sets on every scrape tick |
| `scrapePool` per job, `scrapeLoop` per target | Clean concurrency boundary; pools reload in parallel |
| Parser abstraction over Content-Type | Supports classic text, OpenMetrics, and protobuf with one loop |
| Staleness via prev/cur ref maps | O(1) detection of disappeared series without full set comparison |
